topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled envelope lay on the coffee table, a stark white rectangle against the worn wood. Liam picked it up, his thumb tracing the faint imprint of the sender's address. His breath hitched, a shallow catch in his chest. Across the paper, in bold, impersonal script, were the words: "Moving to Seattle." His best friend, Maya, was moving to Seattle. The silence in the apartment suddenly felt thick, suffocating. He stared at the window, watching the familiar streetlights blur, unable to process the seismic shift that this news represented. His hands started to tremble, and he set the envelope down with a soft thud, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room. He needed to talk to her, to ask questions, but the words seemed to have evaporated before they reached his throat.

[story 2]

"Seattle." The word hung in the air, sharp and cold. Sarah reread the text message for the tenth time, the screen illuminating the stark reality of the situation. She felt a strange tightness constricting her ribs, a physical pressure building behind her sternum. She walked to the kitchen counter, gripping the edge until her knuckles bleached white. Every mundane object—the chipped mug, the stack of unread books—seemed charged with significance, every item a marker of a life about to fracture. She took a long, deliberate sip of water, the coolness doing nothing to quell the churning sensation in her stomach. She didn't want to look at her phone again; the image of Maya laughing in their shared living room felt impossibly distant now.

[story 3]

Mark found the news tucked inside a library book he was returning. The realization hit him like a physical blow, knocking the air from his lungs. He stood motionless in the narrow aisle, the scent of aged paper suddenly overwhelming. His vision tunneled slightly; the fluorescent lights of the bookstore seemed too bright, too harsh. He started pacing, a restless, jerky motion that took him from the narrow stacks to the dusty periodicals, back and forth, seeking some anchor in the familiar chaos of the shelves. He kept running a hand through his hair, feeling the dampness of his palm, a desperate attempt to ground himself against the swirling uncertainty that had just taken root in his chest.
